coredata.dc:title: Attaining the strength-plasticity-electricity balance of carbon nanotube/Cu composites through combining the intragranular carbonized polymer dots distribution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhaojie
You, Xin
Liu, Liang
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Li, Caiju
Tao, Jingmei
Pai, Junjun
Zhao, Wenmin
coredata.dc:description: Insight into the well-balanced mechanical-electrical properties of copper composites is requisite to provide an understanding for the development of novel structure–function integrated materials. In this study, the multi-dimensional hybrid reinforcements, carbon nanotube and zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (abbreviated as: CNT + CPD), were incorporated within copper matrix via spraying pyrolysis dispersion and segmented ball milling routes. A novel strategy was developed to simultaneously introduce intragranular CPD and intergranular CNT by controlling the spatial configuration. Characterization results show that the dispersibility and interface wettability of CNT-Cu were improved by the spraying pyrolysis. Intragranular CPD facilitated the trapping and accumulation of dislocations inside grain interior, which enhanced the strain hardening capability. 3.0 vol% CNT-CPD/Cu composites achieved good strength-plasticity compatibility (410 MPa and 19.8 %, i.e., 146.6 % and 139.3 % of the CNT/Cu composites, respectively). Meanwhile, composites had also displayed good electrical conductivity (96.5% IACS), which was attributed to the creation of conductive graphitic channels locally around intragranular CPD and produced a potent electric field with little charge loss. This work presented an effective route for the design of advanced copper composites and demonstrated potential in applications as progressive multi-functional materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper matrix composites
Carbonized polymer dots (CPD)
Carbon nanotubes
Spraying pyrolysis
Electrical conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CuNPs@CNT and CPD reinforcements
Strengthening and toughening mechanisms of CNT-CPD hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the processing route of CNT-CPD/Cu composites.
(a) TEM image and particles size statistics plot of CPD; (b–c) HRTEM image and related Fourier diffraction pattern of CPD; TEM and HRTEM images (d–g) of CNT/CuxO composites powders.

(a) XRD spectra of CuxO-CNT; (b) FTIR spectra of CuxO-CNT and CPD; (c) full XPS spectrum of CuNPs@CNT and CPD; (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu LM2; (e) XPS C1s spectra for CPD; (f) Raman spectra of reinforcements.

(a–c) Morphologies of reduced CuNPs@CNT reinforcement in different magnification; (d, e) mono-dispersed CNT is connected into a bundle or network by CuNPs, (f) three type models of CuNPs@CNT powders; (g) as-received Cu-based powder, (h–i) CuNPs@CNT-CPD/Cu composite powders after ball milling, and (i) the typical feature that CuNPs@CNT embedded in flake Cu powders.

SEM images and EBSD analysis of CNT-CPD/Cu composites: (a) transverse section and (c) upper surface of CNT-CPD/Cu composites, (b) and (d) corresponding to the high magnification SEM images of (a) and (c), (e) EBSD inverse pole figure plot of the upper surface of CNT-CPD/Cu composites; (f) the grain size statistics plot of (e).

Interfacial microstructure of 3.0 vol% CNT-CPD/Cu composites: (a) TEM image of CNT dispersed in the matrix, (b, c) HRTEM images of the Cu-CNT interface, (d) TEM of CPD clusters dispersed in matrix, (e, f) HRTEM of CPD clusters, (g) EDX line scanning of specified area in (d), (h, h1) FFT and IFFT images of selected region in (f).

(a) Stress–strain curves of the composites and pure Cu, (b) radar diagram of mechanical and electrical properties of the composites and pure Cu, fracture surfaces of (c) pure Cu, (d) CNT/Cu, (e) CPD/Cu, (f) CNT-CPD/Cu; (g, h) high magnified SEM of CNT-CPD/Cu composites shows the bridging and pulled-out features of CNT.

(a, b) Bright field and dark field TEM images of CNT-CPD/Cu composites after tensile tests; (c) IFFT images of marked region in (a); (d) schematic diagram of the microstructure, reinforcement and toughening effects of CNT/Cu, CPD/Cu and CNT-CPD/Cu composites.
(a) EC of the composites and pure Cu in this work; (b) a comparison of the EC and UTS with previously reported Cu-based composites [16,50–54].
Electrical conductivity and tensile properties of pure Cu and the composites.

head.ce:title: Attaining the strength-plasticity-electricity balance of carbon nanotube/Cu composites through combining the intragranular carbonized polymer dots distribution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi, 341000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Insight into the well-balanced mechanical-electrical properties of copper composites is requisite to provide an understanding for the development of novel structure–function integrated materials. In this study, the multi-dimensional hybrid reinforcements, carbon nanotube and zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (abbreviated as: CNT + CPD), were incorporated within copper matrix via spraying pyrolysis dispersion and segmented ball milling routes. A novel strategy was developed to simultaneously introduce intragranular CPD and intergranular CNT by controlling the spatial configuration. Characterization results show that the dispersibility and interface wettability of CNT-Cu were improved by the spraying pyrolysis. Intragranular CPD facilitated the trapping and accumulation of dislocations inside grain interior, which enhanced the strain hardening capability. 3.0 vol% CNT-CPD/Cu composites achieved good strength-plasticity compatibility (410 MPa and 19.8 %, i.e., 146.6 % and 139.3 % of the CNT/Cu composites, respectively). Meanwhile, composites had also displayed good electrical conductivity (96.5% IACS), which was attributed to the creation of conductive graphitic channels locally around intragranular CPD and produced a potent electric field with little charge loss. This work presented an effective route for the design of advanced copper composites and demonstrated potential in applications as progressive multi-functional materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the superior mechanical and physical properties, carbon nanomaterials (graphene, GR and carbon nanotubes, CNT), have been deemed ideal reinforcements for metal matrix composites (MMCs) [1–4]. In recent years, a great quantity studies have been conducted on nanocarbon reinforced MMCs by various methods, such as spray pyrolysis (SP) [5], ball milling (BM) [6,7], molecular level mixing (MLM) [8–10] and electro-deposition [11,12]. Incorporation of CNT or GR has been proven to enhance strength and stimulate attractive comprehensive performance [13,14].

Generally, MMCs exhibits unilateral performance improvement, it is accompanied by a worsening of other physical properties, like electrical conductivity (EC) and thermal properties. For instance, Han et al. [15] dispersed graphene nanosheets within Al powders by mechanical alloying method, and the strength of resultant composites was 2.07 times greater than pure Al. However, the plasticity, electrical and thermal properties had degraded evidently. Daoush et al. [16] found that the strength of CNT/Cu composites increased with high CNT content, but the EC was decreased gradually. Thus, the “barrel effect” has impeded the wide engineering application of MMCs. It appears that there is an upper limit to improving the combination performance via the single reinforcement. To achieve further improvements in comprehensive performance, it is crucial to regulate the microstructure of MMCs or adopt the synergetic reinforcements from some novel perspectives [17–19]. More recently, several researchers have tried to hybridize different dimensional of nano-fillers to promote more effective enhancements in MMCs. Chen et al. [20] built an Al2O3-CNT hybrid structure by accumulative roll-compositing, resulting in dual-reinforced Cu composites and exhibited better comprehensive properties than individual counterparts. Zhang et al. [21] prepared CNT-GN hybrid reinforced copper composites, CNT was well-dispersed in copper matrix due to the pegging effect of GN, leading to further improved mechanical properties. Therefore, it can be concluded that the multi-dimensional synergistic enhancement strategy is a promising approach to achieve a good synergistic equilibrium of strength-ductility compatibility and electrical conductivity for MMCs.

The rise of advanced nanocarbon materials specifically zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots (0D, CPD), possessing a unique core–shell structure and remarkable electrical properties [22,23]. To some extent, it provides new options for materials design and filling the voids of 0D to 2D nanocarbon reinforcements. Characterized by the unique 0D quantum size effect, CPD is easily driven into the grain interior, trapping dislocation loops, and results in more effective Orowan strengthening in MMCs [24,25]. Our previous reports have represented an essential step in overcoming several major difficulties of CPD/Cu nanocomposites via the “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy. The intragranular dispersion of CPD has proven to be a viable means of achieving enhanced work hardening and thus high ductility, while also enhancing EC of CPD/Cu composites [22,26–29]. Therefore, it is prospective to break through the mechanical-electrical property trade-off in CNT/Cu composites via the incorporation of zero-dimensional carbonized polymer dots. However, investigations about the CPD-contained hybrid reinforcements have an insufficient appreciation for the relationship between the interfacial structure and properties. In this work, CNT decorated with Cu nanoparticles (NPs) was prepared by spraying pyrolysis method to further increase the dispersion and wettability in copper matrix. Cu-based composites reinforced with collaborative reinforcements (CPD and CNT) were prepared via BM and spark plasma sintering (SPS). We designed a new tactic to concurrently introduce intragranular CPD and intergranular CNT by controlling the spatial configuration, overcoming the insufficient intrinsic properties of the single specific type and integrating the individual merits of the components. CNT-CPD/Cu composites had attained synergetic equilibrium of good strength-ductility compatibility and electrical conductivity. Our present study aimed to pinpoint the detailed structure–property correlation in CNT-CPD/Cu composites, as well as revealed the response of CPD-contained hybrid to the strengthening mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CuNPs@CNT and CPD reinforcements
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, Cu nanoparticles decorated CNT (CuNPs@CNT) powders were synthesized via the spraying pyrolysis and thermal reduction methods (Fig. 1 (a)) [30]. Typically, 9.0 g Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O (CuAc) was dissolved in 5 L distilled water and stirred for 1 h to prepare CuAc solution. CNT weighing 5.0 g was dispersed ultrasonically in the above solution, and then the prepared CNT-CuAc precursor solution was moved to the ultrasonic atomization system and atomized into small droplets at a speed of 3 L/h. Simultaneously, controlled argon gas with a fixed flow rate of 100 mL/min was utilized to transport the droplets into the tube furnace and heat-decomposed the droplets to produce CNT-CuxO composites powders (the pyrolysis temperature was 700 °C). Subsequently, the collected CNT-CuxO composites powders was reduced at 280 °C for 5 h under H2 to obtain the CuNPs@CNT powders.

On the other hand, CPD was synthesized via the “bottom-up” one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1(b)) [31,32]. Detailly, 6.0 g citric acid (C6H8O7, CA) and 1.5 mL ethylenediamine (EDA) were solubilized in 90 mL deionized water. After that, the reactor was insulated at 160 °C for 5 h. The synthesized solution was added into a 1 kDa dialysis tube membrane and then allowed for 72 h of dialysis to remove the residual organic matter. By means of a freeze-drying technology, the fluffy CPD powders was obtained.

The schematic illustrated in Fig. 1(c) indicates the CNT-CPD/Cu composites preparation process. Firstly, CuNPs@CNT (1.5 vol%) and CPD (1.5 vol%) powders were ultrasonically dispersed in ethanol. Copper powders and hybrid reinforcements were BM at 150 rpm for 10 h, then at 300 rpm for 6 h to obtain the (CuNPs@CNT-CPD)/Cu composites powders. Finally, the powders was consolidated via SPS at 873 K for 8 min. Correspondingly, bulk composites obtained from the (CuNPs@CNT-CPD)/Cu powders were denoted by CNT-CPD/Cu hereafter. For comparison, pure Cu, 3.0vol% CNT/Cu and 3.0 vol% CPD/Cu composites were also prepared with the same procedure.

The phase components of composites powders were characterized by XRD. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific iN10) was used to characterize the functional groups present on the surfaces of CPD and CNT. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Gemini 300) was used to characterize the microstructures of powders and composites. Electron Backscattered Diffraction (EBSD, FEI Nova NanoSEM 430) analysis was conducted to assess the average grain size of composites. Transmission electron microscope (TEM, Thermo F200s) was used to observe the morphology of reinforcements and the interface microstructures of bulk composites. Tensile properties were assessed by a universal testing machine, with a crosshead speed of 0.18 mm/min.

Fig. 2 displays the microstructures of as-synthesized CPD and CuxO-CNT powders. CPD has a spherical shape and the average diameter was estimated to be 2.69 nm (Fig. 2(a)). Fig. 2(b and c) demonstrate a majority of CPD that shows clear crystal lattice with plane spacing of 0.21 nm, suggesting a standard graphite (002) plane. TEM images in Fig. 2(d and e) display that after spray pyrolysis treatment, CNT retains their typical tubular shapes and uniformly coated by nanoparticles. During the spray pyrolysis process, the atomized CNT-CuAc precursor droplets (usually have a mean size of ∼30 μm) were carried by N2 flows and moved through the elongated heating zone. The refined precursor droplets experienced sufficient thermal decomposition and fully transformed into the copper oxide (CuxO) nanoparticles, exactly as the “flash-evaporation” process. HRTEM image in Fig. 2(f) confirms that some CuxO nanoparticles are identified as the Cu2O particles. Nanoparticles (NPs) tightly integrates with CNT and displays the “necklace-like structure”, which impedes the CNT aggregation and benefits for their dispersibility. Furthermore, it is assumed that these nanoparticles can serve as bridge that connects CNT and matrix, facilitating to form strong interface adhesion [33].

XRD was employed to clarify the phase components of composite powders synthesized by spraying pyrolysis. CuAc was decomposed into Cu2O and CuO after the pyrolysis process. Besides, the peak at 25.2° corresponding to the typical graphite phase of (111) crystal plane can be clearly observed in enlarged view, which proves the existence of CNT. The functional groups on CuxO-CNT and CPD are recognized based on FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 (b)). There are several distinct absorption peaks, indicating the presence of C–N (∼1215 cm−1), CO (∼1715 cm−1), C–H (∼2945 cm−1), N–H, O–H (∼3445 cm−1 and ∼1410 cm−1) bands. The existence of these functional groups on CNT and CPD facilitates effective stability and hydrophilicity in polar solvents, thereby contributing to their dispersibility [34].

Fig. 3(c–e) show the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of CuNPs@CNT and CPD. XPS full-spectrum in Fig. 3(c) reveals four XPS peaks of Cu 2p3, Cu LM2, C1s and O1s in CuNPs@CNT composite powders, which suggests that the atomic ratio of 17 % and 83 % for Cu and C atoms. High-resolution XPS of the Cu LM2 peak (Fig. 3(d)) indicates Cu LM2 peak at 952.4 eV and 932.5 eV which correspond to Cu2P1/2 and Cu2P3/2 of Cu, while no peaks of CuO or Cu2O can be seen, suggesting that CuO and Cu2O have nearly been reduced to Cu. XPS full-spectrum quantificationally estimate the contents of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in CPD (Fig. 3(c)), which was 61.0 %, 10.5 % and 28.9 %, respectively. High-resolution XPS of the C1s peak (Fig. 3(e)) displays that the C1s peaks at 284.19 eV, 285.04 eV, 286.39 eV, 288.16 eV, and 288.98 eV, which correspond to carbon in the form of CC, C–C, C–N/C–O, CN/CO and OC–O, respectively [35]. The ratio of sp3 (30.92 %) and sp2 (9.83 %) is 19/6, manifesting that the synthesized CPD contains amorphous defects [36]. Raman spectrums of CPD and CNT are illustrated in Fig. 3(f), two characteristic peaks are identified near 1580 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1, which correspond the G peak and D peak, respectively [37]. The ID/IG values of CPD and CNT are 0.88 and 0.92, demonstrating the similar structural integrity of crystalline carbon.

SEM images in Fig. 4 (a–c) displays the morphologies of hydrogen reduced CuNPs@CNT reinforcement and (CuNPs@CNT-CPD)/Cu composites powders. It reveals that after reduction treatment, CNT displays uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation. The enlarged tube diameter and roughened surface of CNT is caused by the coated CuNPs. Some individual CNT is bound by large CuNPs and has formed slight CNT bundles or CNT networks (Fig. 4d–e), the models of different CuNPs@CNT bundles are shown in Fig. 4(f). Noting that CNT bundles are differ from randomly arranged CNT clusters, it is assumed that these bundles could release higher strengthening abilities. Fig. 4(g) displays the morphology of the as-received Cu-based powder, indicating that the powder composes of dendritic particles and has large specific surface area. After ball milling, Cu-based powder has transformed into flaky shapes (Fig. 4(h)). It would enhance the geometric compatibility between Cu powders and CNT or CPD, as illustrated in Fig. 4(i).

Fig. 5(a) displays the microstructure of the transverse section of CNT-CPD/Cu composites. The flake powders are self-assembly alignment and the highly ordered laminated structure is obtained. CNT is surrounded by laminated matrix, which forms a “brick-and-mortar” structure [38]. The upper surface of CNT-CPD/Cu in Fig. 5 (c) shows that CNT distributes uniformly in the grain boundaries (GB) and grains interior. Inverse pole figures (IPF) in Fig. 5(e) confirms the absence of texture formation. CNT-CPD/Cu composites exhibits equiaxed and coarse grains with randomized orientations, and its average grain size is ∼1.24 μm smaller than that of pure Cu (3.31 μm). It indicates that the combination of CNT and CPD significantly refines Cu grains.

The typical TEM images in Fig. 6 reveal the reinforcements’ morphology and interface structure of CNT-CPD/Cu composites. CNT is observed alongside the GB while CPD clusters are uniformly distributed within grain (Fig. 6(a)). HRTEM (Fig. 6(c)) confirms the clean and straight CNT-Cu interface without any gaps or voids, moreover, CNT exhibits high crystallinity. Additionally, the average diameter of CPD clusters is 51 ± 25 nm, most of CPD clusters are located within grain interiors, illustrated in Fig. 6(d). The intragranular CPD distribution is achieved by GB migration and separation from CPD cluster [39]. Compared with larger sized CPD clusters, the smaller size significantly reduces the Zener force, and this facilitates CPD clusters inside the grains. Fig. 6(g) shows the EDS analysis on the cluster, revealing an opposite trend in the content of Cu and C elements, implying individual CPD gathers at these CPD clusters. Fig. 6(e) displays the structure of CPD clusters (white dashed box in Fig. d), it has further confirmed that CPD clusters consists of much CPD particles, just like lots of graphene dots embedded within a giant carbon network.

Detailed interface characteristic between CPD and Cu matrix is characterized by HRTEM (Fig. 6(f)). It seems that the interface adhesion is robust, and a transition layer has been formed. FFT and IFFT images obtained from region-h are used to analyze the interfacial compositions. There are two sets of diffraction spots corresponding to (
2 ‾
10) and (111) crystal planes of Cu2O, indicating the formation of transitional intermixing near the CPD-Cu interface (Fig. 6(f)). The transition interface of Cu2O results from the mutual diffusion between Cu and O atoms derived from CPD defects/edges [40]. The transition zone facilitates strong interface cohesion of CPD-Cu by converting the mechanical bonding to the chemical type [41,42].

Engineering stress–strain curves of composites and pure Cu are displayed in Fig. 7 (a), with detailed statistics listed in Table 1 . Encouragingly, it can be found that CNT-CPD/Cu composites exhibits excellent ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 411 MPa and yield strength (YS) of 340 MPa, remarkably greater than those of pure Cu (UTS: 241 MPa, YS: 185 MPa), CNT/Cu (UTS: 295 MPa, YS: 263 MPa) and CPD/Cu (UTS: 298 MPa, YS: 242 MPa). It demonstrates that remarkable strengthening has obtained through the synergistic effect of CNT and CPD. CPD + CNT hybrid show a greater increment than the sum of their single strength contributions. In contrast to pure Cu, both YS and UTS of CPD-CNT/Cu are increased by 155 MPa and 170 MPa, meanwhile, the elongation maintains ∼19.8 %, which implies excellent strength-ductility synergy. This synergistic effect is mainly attributable to the intragranular CPD particles and elevated load-transfer strengthening by CuNPs@CNT. A deep understanding of strengthening and toughening mechanisms from CPD + CNT reinforcements would be investigated in the discussion section.

Comparations between electrical conductivity, mechanical properties and reinforcement types in Cu composites were concluded in a schematic radar diagram (Fig. 7(b)). The hardness and strength of CNT/Cu composites improve markedly, but the EC and elongation have degraded seriously. On the contrary, the elongation and EC of CPD/Cu composites increase significantly. However, CNT-CPD/Cu has combined these advantages, achieving the complementarity of CNT and CPD performance benefits. Compared with other reported Cu composites [43–45], such as La–Al2O3/Cu, GR-TiB2/Cu, TiC-CNT/Cu composites, the present CNT-CPD/Cu composites outperform other references in avoiding the strength-conductivity trade-off. Therefore, CPD + CNT provides new insights into the design of advanced Cu composites for simultaneously improved electrical and mechanical performances.

Fig. 7(c–h) depicts fractography of composites and pure Cu, obviously, the broadened and deep dimples ensure the classic ductile fracture, which coincides with the excellent fracture elongation. Based on the fracture mechanism, the addition of reinforcement leads to the appearance of stress-concentrated areas, which leads to reduced dimple size. Such observation is related to the deteriorative ductility of CNT/Cu composites. However, the intragranular CPD distribution contributes to the mitigation of stress concentration and facilitates the uniform elongation of CPD/Cu and CNT-CPD/Cu composites. The dimple size of CPD/Cu composites is large and becomes more homogeneous. The enlarged fractography of CNT-CPD/Cu composites is presented in Fig. 7(g–h), most of the ruptured CNT in the fractural surface possesses a sharp tip. Meanwhile, the exposed CNT is discovered to bridge over both sides of the ruptured Cu matrix. The bridging and fractured failure behaviors suggest that CNT had efficiently transferred loads from copper matrix via robust interfacial bonding, as well as induced bridging effects to hinder the breakage of composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strengthening and toughening mechanisms of CNT-CPD hybrid
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, from the grain size statistics results (Fig. 5), we can draw a persuasive conclusion that the incorporation of CPD-CNT hybrid reinforcements significantly refines the grains. Secondly, the synergistic reinforcement effects of CNT-CPD/Cu composites displays an outstanding integration of high strength and ductility, which attributes to the strong interface bonding of CNT-Cu and intragranular CPD distribution. Most of the exposed CNT exhibits a sharp tip as shown by the fracture surfaces of the CNT-CPD/Cu composites (Fig. 7), which provides convincing evidence for the load transfer effect of CNT. Since the aspect ratio of CPD is close to 1, the load transfer caused by CPD is negligible. Moreover, the design of distributing the CPD within the grain contributes to trap and store dislocations, resulting in enhanced strain hardening rate by the Orowan strengthening. Combined with the above analysis, the contribution of CPD-CNT hybrid enhancements are evaluated. The potential mechanisms for enhancing the strength of CNT-CPD/Cu composites include grain refinement (
Δ σ
G R
), load transfer (
Δ σ
L T
) and Orowan strengthening (
Δ σ
O r o w a n
). The YS of composite (
σ
y c
) can be calculated using the following equation:
(1)
σ
y c
=
σ
y m
+
Δ σ
G R
+
Δ σ
L T
+
Δ σ
O r o w a n
where
σ
y m
is the YS of the Cu matrix. It can be noticed that the experimental YS (340 MPa) exhibits good agreement with calculated values (347.1 MPa), indicating that reinforcement effects of CNT-CPD/Cu composites are dominated by load transfer strengthening (64.2 MPa), Orowan strengthening (72.6 MPa) and grain refinement strengthening (25.3 MPa) (Detailed calculations are presented in the supplementary material).

To better investigate the toughening mechanism, TEM characterization of the post-deformed CNT-CPD/Cu composites was carried out. Fig. 8 (a–c) display the microstructure of post-deformed CNT-CPD/Cu composites, where abundant dislocations can be observed near CNT and CPD indicating that the dislocations are not only hindered at the CNT-Cu interface (Fig. S1) but also captured by intragranular CPD. IFFT images of the interface areas display that dislocation loops are detected around the intragranular CPD (Fig. 8(c)), which shows obvious CPD-dislocation interaction. When the dislocation line passes through or bypasses the second phase particles, the dislocation line is bent, which increases the resistance of dislocation movement, and provides favorable location for storing dislocations [46]. The intragranular CPD will initiate rigid interfacial region in the grain, this not only provide a large trap capacity for anchored on and thus storing dislocations but also serve as source site for dislocation occurring. Consequently, during deformation, the kinetic equilibrium regarding dislocation proliferation and annihilation occurs at the CPD/Cu interface resulting in a more persistent uniform elongation, contributing to the strength-toughness synergy.

Based on the analysis above, the toughening and strengthening behaviors of the composites is illustrated in Fig. 8(d). In the case of CNT singly reinforced composites, the fine-grained strengthening introduced by the pegging effect of CNT on the GB as well as elevated load-transfer strengthening help to the high strength. But in the Kelly-Tyson model, dislocations pegged by CNT and stored at the interfaces promote crack initiation [47], which will lead to premature failure of Cu/CNT composites. Further movement of dislocations is difficult in this case. However, due to the Orowan mechanism in CPD/Cu composites, dislocations can pass by the CPD and remain mobile in the matrix. Consequently, further deformation of the copper matrix becomes feasible. In contrast single component, the utilization of hybrid reinforcements overcomes the inadequacies inherent in a single specified type and combines the unique advantages of each components. The increased grain refinement strengthening, intragranular CPD introduced Orowan strengthening and elevated load-transfer strengthening by CuNPs decorated CNT help to enhance the strength of CNT-CPD/Cu composites. Additionally, CNT-CPD hybrid will significantly promote the dislocation storage capability, which is essential for improving the work hardening ability and hence ductility. Similarly, the bridging and pull-out of CNT can produce additional toughening effects through crack bridging, passivation and deflection [48], which consumes significant energy and delay the neck during breaking. Accordingly, this study suggests that by adjusting the spatial distribution and dimension of multi-scale synergetic reinforcements, provides new strategy for designing composites with enhanced strength and deformability.

Previous reports indicate that it is extremely difficult to mitigate the conflict between EC and mechanical performance for copper composites [49]. Comparisons of CNT-CPD/Cu composites with related works from other studies are illustrated in Fig. 9 (b) [16,50–54]. For instance, Chen et al. [50] found that 0.4 vol% GNPs contributed to 201 % improvement in YS while the EC decreased from 95 % IACS to 80 % IACS. Akbarpour et al. [51] distributed CNT in copper matrix by BM, the UTS of CNT/Cu composites was increased by 120 %. However, the EC was reduced by significantly. It seems that the reinforcement mechanisms of the mechanical/electrical properties are contradictory.

However, the findings of this study demonstrate that the EC of CNT-CPD/Cu composites surpasses that of the CNT/Cu composites (Fig. 9(a)), this suggests that CPD plays a crucial role in facilitating charge transfer. In accordance with Matthiessen's law, dislocation, GB scattering and interfacial scattering might predetermine high electrical resistance [53]. The presence of extra charge transfer paths is hinted at the above microstructure, as the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 6(f). This was demonstrated by the creation of locally conductive graphitic channels around the nano-sized CPD and producing a potent electric field with little charge loss [54]. In addition, a clean and robust interface can effectively reduce interfacial impedance caused by electron scattering near the intragranular CPD interface. Nevertheless, the distribution of CNT along GBs leads to nonnegligible charge loss and elevated electrical resistance. This can be attributed to the pronounced GB scattering effect, resulting in the low EC of CNT/Cu composites. Additionally, CPD plays a bridging role in the charge transfer process from CNT to Cu, while the π-π conjugation interaction between CNT and CPD can greatly enhance EC by effectively accelerating the interfacial charge transfer rate [55]. Summarily, the CNT-CPD hybrid enhancements have improved the mechanical and electrical conductivity of bulk composites concurrently.

In this work, the multi-dimensional hybrid reinforcements (CNT + CPD), were incorporated within copper matrix via spraying pyrolysis dispersion and segmented ball milling routes. A novel strategy was developed to simultaneously introduce intragranular CPD and intergranular CNT by controlling the spatial configuration, which actualized the synergetic equilibrium of good strength-ductility compatibility and electrical conductivity of the composites. The main conclusions can be summarized below:
1)
CPD dispersed inside grains was benefit for capturing and accumulating dislocations, thereby improving the work hardening capability through Orowan strengthening.
2)
The EL and UTS of CNT-CPD/Cu composites possessed maximum values of 19.8 % and 411 MPa, which were 46.6 % and 39.3 % higher than those of CNT/Cu composites respectively, acquiring an excellent strength-ductility synergy.
3)
The contributions to the improved strength of composites mainly come from grain refinement strengthening, intragranular CPD introduced Orowan strengthening and elevated load-transfer strengthening by CNT. The excellent plasticity originates from the introduction of intragranular CPD to promote increased dislocation storage capacity as well as the bridging and pull-out effects induced by CNT.
4)
Meanwhile, the electrical conductivity of CNT-CPD/Cu composites (96.5 % IACS) was far better than that of CNT/Cu (86.7 % IACS), indicating that CPD serves an essential role in facilitating charge transfer.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52174345, 52064032). Major Science and Technology Projects of Yunnan Province (Grant No. 202202AG050004). Science and technology project of Jiangxi Provincial Education Department (No. 204201400859).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Pan, S. Q. Xiao, X. Lu, et al. Fabrication, mechanical properties and electrical conductivity of Al2O3 reinforced Cu/CNTs composites, Journal of Alloys and Compounds 782 (2019) 1015-1023, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.12.222.
